scenario: A team of 8 developers is collaborating on a complex BPMN process across three Camunda 8 SaaS environments (dev, staging, production). They have a Web Modeler project containing the BPMN file plus several DMN decisions and Forms. The team lead wants to: 1. Restrict who can edit the model (some are reviewers, some are editors) 2. Track changes with a clear version history 3. Allow simultaneous editing on different parts of the model
question: Which Web Modeler features support this collaboration setup?

options.text: md:27-32), version history with snapshots and named versions, and real-time collaborative editing with conflict awareness. For the team's requirements: role-based editing restrictions are satisfied by assigning Viewer or Commenter roles to reviewers and Editor roles to developers who need to make changes; version history satisfies the change tracking requirement by recording edit history and allowing named milestones; real-time collaboration lets multiple developers work simultaneously on different parts of the model with live cursor visibility. The Project Admin can also manage team membership and environment-specific deployment permissions.

Web Modeler is single-user -- concurrent editing must be managed via Git locking. This is factually incorrect. Camunda 8 Web Modeler is a cloud-based collaborative tool designed for multi-user real-time editing, similar to Google Docs or Figma. Multiple team members can be working in the same BPMN diagram simultaneously, with live cursor tracking and real-time change propagation. There is no Git locking mechanism in Web Modeler -- it manages concurrency internally.

Permissions are configured at the cluster level only; per-project access control is unavailable. This misidentifies where Web Modeler access control lives. md:27-32. ), not who can edit BPMN models in Web Modeler. The two access control layers are independent and serve different purposes.

Web Modeler supports version history only via integration with external Git; native versioning requires a Git repository. That premise is false. 0 Published", "hotfix-2026-05-20") that act as stable reference points in the version history. Teams can view the history of changes, compare versions, and restore previous states without any external Git integration. Git integration is an optional additional capability for teams that want bi-directional sync with source control repositories, but it is not a requirement for version history functionality.

a.text: Correct. Web Modeler supports the full collaboration model: per-project access controls assigning four roles to team members per web-modeler/collaboration/collaboration.md:27-32 -- Project Admin (edit project itself + invite users), Editor (edit folders/diagrams), Commenter (often missed: view + comment only, no edit), Viewer (read-only) -- version history with both auto-saves and named milestones (e.g., "v1.0 published to production"), and real-time multi-user editing with conflict awareness (similar to Google Docs -- multiple users see each other's cursors and edits live). Note: any answer listing only 3 roles (omitting Commenter) is incomplete.

b.text: Incorrect. Web Modeler is explicitly multi-user with real-time collaboration.

c.text: Incorrect. Permissions are project-level, not just cluster-level.

d.text: Incorrect. Web Modeler has native version history; Git integration is supplementary, not required.

explanation: Web Modeler supports the full collaboration model: per-project access controls assigning four roles to team members per web-modeler/collaboration/collaboration.md:27-32 -- Project Admin (edit project itself + invite users), Editor (edit folders/diagrams), Commenter (often missed: view + comment only, no edit), Viewer (read-only) -- version history with both auto-saves and named milestones (e.g., "v1.0 published to production"), and real-time multi-user editing with conflict awareness (similar to Google Docs -- multiple users see each other's cursors and edits live). Note: any answer listing only 3 roles (omitting Commenter) is incomplete.
